note.soap:
S: 35-year-old patient. The patient reports a 5-day history of bilateral facial pressure accompanied by headache, postnasal drip, and yellow nasal discharge. They also note a change in smell and severe one-sided facial pain. There is no fever, no double worsening of symptoms, no severe onset, no eye swelling or vision changes, no dental pain, and no systemic symptoms such as confusion, stiff neck, or trouble breathing. Allergy features are present. Patient is not pregnant.

O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. Vital signs and physical findings are not available. No signs of orbital involvement or systemic infection can be assessed remotely.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Acute bacterial sinusitis  
  - Differential: Viral upper respiratory infection, allergic rhinosinusitis, migraine headache  
  - Red flags considered: No fever, no vision changes, no eye swelling, no confusion, no stiff neck, no severe one-sided facial pain onset, no double worsening of symptoms

P:  
  - Disposition: Urgent care or primary care follow-up recommended if symptoms worsen or fail to improve in 3–5 days.  
  - Plan: Recommend symptomatic treatment with nasal saline irrigation and intranasal corticosteroids if tolerated. Consider a short course of oral antibiotics if symptoms persist beyond 10 days or worsen, given the presence of yellow nasal discharge, facial pressure, and smell change. Over-the-counter analgesics for headache and facial pain are advised. Avoid decongestants if contraindicated.  
  - Safety net: Advise patient to seek immediate care if they develop fever, worsening facial pain, swelling around the eyes, vision changes, confusion, or difficulty breathing.

Evidence (snippets used):  
- Duration 5 days  
- Bilateral facial pressure, headache, postnasal drip  
- Yellow nasal discharge  
- Smell change  
- Severe one-sided facial pain  
- No fever, no double worsening, no severe onset  
- No eye swelling, no vision change  
- Allergy features present  
- No confusion, no stiff neck, no trouble breathing  
- Pregnancy possible: no; Patient is not pregnant
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.
